words.definition: a soft fabric that feels like velvet
long hairs that grow near an animal's mouth
having moving parts that work like a machine
normal; nothing special
knowledge and good judgment
the lines where pieces of fabric are sewn together
telling the truth; not lying
places where two parts connect and can move
the time when someone goes to sleep
feeling angry because someone has something you want
exciting experiences or activities
looked around and discovered new places
when flowers open up and show their colors
holes in the ground where animals live
to look at something closely to learn about it
very surprised and impressed
feeling puzzled or mixed up
smelled something by breathing in through the nose
when someone's body gets very hot because they are sick
help someone feel better when they are sad or scared
began to grow from the ground
a magical being with special powers
a feeling like tiny prickles on your skin
parts inside the body that help you move

questions.question: What holiday was it in the story?
What presents did the Boy receive besides the velveteen rabbit?
Where did Nana put the velveteen rabbit?
Why were the mechanical toys proud of themselves?
Who was kind to the velveteen rabbit in the nursery?
How was the Skin Horse different from the other toys?
What did the Skin Horse say Real isn't about?
How does someone become Real, according to the Skin Horse?
Why did Nana give the Boy the velveteen rabbit one evening?
What did the Boy say about the rabbit when he held him?
What season came to the house and garden?
Where did the rabbit go during the Boy's adventures?
What did the Boy make in the dirt for his rabbit?
What important word did the Boy use to describe his rabbit?
Where was the velveteen rabbit left one evening?
What creatures came out of the raspberry bushes?
What did the real rabbits discover about the velveteen rabbit?
How did the real rabbits know he wasn't really real?
What happened to the Boy after the sad evening with the rabbits?
How did the velveteen rabbit help during the Boy's illness?
What happened when the rabbit's tear fell to the ground?
Who was the beautiful fairy that appeared?
What happened to the rabbit after the fairy's kiss?
How was the rabbit different after the magic?

informationalTexts.content:
Learning about Margery Williams biography helps us understand the world better. This topic connects to the story we're reading and teaches important concepts. By exploring different subjects, we become better students and more knowledgeable people.

When you study Margery Williams biography, think about how it relates to your own life. Ask questions, make connections, and share what you learn with others. Every topic we study helps us grow and discover new interests.

questions.question: Why is Margery Williams biography important?
questions.answer: it helps us understand the world

questions.question: What should you do when studying?
questions.answer: ask questions and make connections

grammarLessons.content:
**Nouns** are words that name people, places, things, or ideas.

**Examples:**
• People: teacher, doctor, friend
• Places: school, park, library
• Things: book, pencil, computer
• Ideas: happiness, courage, freedom

**Practice:** Find 3 nouns in today's story!

**Verbs** are action words that tell what someone or something does.

**Examples:**
• run, jump, dance, sing
• think, feel, hope, dream
• was, were, is, are (linking verbs)

**Practice:** Circle the verbs in these sentences from the story.

**Adjectives** describe nouns. They tell us more details about people, places, or things.

**Examples:**
• beautiful princess
• dark forest
• brave knight

**Practice:** Add adjectives to make these sentences more interesting!

**Pronouns**

This lesson teaches about Pronouns. Look for examples in today's story.

**Practice:** Apply what you learned!

**Compound Sentences**

This lesson teaches about Compound Sentences. Look for examples in today's story.

**Practice:** Apply what you learned!

**Capitalization**

This lesson teaches about Capitalization. Look for examples in today's story.

**Practice:** Apply what you learned!

languageLessons.content:
**ABC Order** (alphabetical order) helps us organize words and find them in dictionaries.

**Steps:**
1. Look at the first letter
2. If first letters match, look at second letter
3. Keep going until you find the difference

**Practice:** Put these words from the story in ABC order.

**Rhyming words** sound the same at the end.

**Examples:**
• cat, hat, mat, sat
• sing, ring, wing, king

**Practice:** Find pairs of rhyming words in today's story!

**Synonyms**

This lesson teaches about Synonyms. Look for examples in today's story.

**Practice:** Apply what you learned!

**Antonyms**

This lesson teaches about Antonyms. Look for examples in today's story.

**Practice:** Apply what you learned!

**Plural Nouns**

This lesson teaches about Plural Nouns. Look for examples in today's story.

**Practice:** Apply what you learned!

**Context Clues**

This lesson teaches about Context Clues. Look for examples in today's story.

**Practice:** Apply what you learned!

writingPrompts.prompt: Write about a time you felt like Velveteen Rabbit.
If you could visit the English nursery, countryside, what would you do?
What is the most important lesson in this story?
Describe your favorite character and why you like them.
How would you solve the problem in this story?
Write a different ending for this story.

journalPrompts.prompt: What do you think will happen next in the story?
Which character are you most like? Why?
What is your favorite part of the story so far?
If you were in this story, what would you do?
What surprised you in this chapter?
How did this story make you feel?
